refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-003 Refined: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and tele-operators to achieve a measured energy efficiency of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (customer-defined threshold) over the agreed evaluation period, through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and real-time feedback mechanisms.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks explicit linkage to the mechanisms (monitoring, control, feedback) that enable efficiency achievement. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is not a passive property but an active capability requiring measurement, optimization logic, and operator awareness. This supports both direct operation and teleoperation modes.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton = total_energy_consumed / total_tonnage_moved over a defined evaluation period (e.g., 40-hour work week). Verify that measured value ≤ E_target. Collect telemetry from Vehicle power systems and cross-reference with load/distance data. Conduct controlled trials under representative worksite conditions with both on-site and tele-operated modes.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) from all propulsion and auxiliary systems.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log tonnage moved (mass × distance or equivalent work metric).
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and display real-time energy_per_ton ratio to operators and tele-operators.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide control recommendations or automated efficiency optimizations (e.g., speed modulation, load balancing, idle reduction) to minimize energy_per_ton.
CR-NGHE-003.5: The NGHE solution shall transmit energy efficiency telemetry and alerts to Site Manager and Maintenance stakeholders for performance tracking and predictive maintenance.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized Energy Management System (EMS) that aggregates real-time power and load telemetry from Vehicle subsystems, computes energy_per_ton in real-time, and broadcasts efficiency metrics and optimization recommendations via Telemetry interface to all stakeholders.

DP-B: Distribute energy monitoring logic across Vehicle onboard controllers; aggregate results at Teleoperation gateway; compute energy_per_ton centrally and push results back to Vehicle and operator displays via Tasking and Telemetry interfaces.

DP-C: Implement energy efficiency as a closed-loop control function within Vehicle autonomous systems; measure power and load locally; compute energy_per_ton; adjust propulsion parameters (speed, torque) autonomously; report results via Telemetry to external stakeholders for oversight only.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement a centralized Energy Management System (EMS) that aggregates real-time power and load telemetry from Vehicle subsystems, computes energy_per_ton in real-time, and broadcasts efficiency metrics and optimization recommendations via Telemetry interface to all stakeholders (Site Manager, Operator, Tele-operator, Maintenance).

potential_sources: EMS depends on reliable Telemetry interface for power and load data ingestion; if Telemetry is delayed or lossy, energy_per_ton computation becomes inaccurate.
EMS must coordinate with Vehicle propulsion control to implement efficiency recommendations; loose coupling via Tasking interface mitigates direct dependency.
EMS output (efficiency metrics) feeds multiple stakeholders; broadcast via Telemetry reduces point-to-point coupling.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A exhibits acceptable coupling: it centralizes the energy efficiency logic (high cohesion) and uses well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking) for external communication. Decoupling is achieved through asynchronous telemetry push rather than synchronous request-response. DP-B introduces distributed logic (higher coupling risk); DP-C embeds efficiency control in Vehicle autonomy (tight coupling, harder to audit for safety). DP-A is preferred for auditability and stakeholder transparency.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle power and load sensors are calibrated and provide sufficient accuracy (±5% or better) for energy_per_ton computation.
Assumption: Telemetry interface has sufficient bandwidth and latency (<1 second) to support real-time energy_per_ton updates.
Risk: If sensor calibration drifts over time, energy_per_ton measurements become unreliable; mitigation: implement periodic sensor validation and alert operators to recalibration needs.

Risk: If EMS computation is CPU-intensive, real-time performance may degrade under high-frequency telemetry; mitigation: profile and optimize EMS algorithms; consider edge computing on Vehicle if needed.

Risk: High safety criticality requires that energy efficiency recommendations do not conflict with safety constraints (e.g., speed reduction for efficiency must not compromise braking or stability); mitigation: Safety Services module must validate all EMS recommendations before transmission to Vehicle.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly include a Safety Services validation gate: 'EMS shall compute and recommend efficiency optimizations; all recommendations shall be validated by Safety Services module before transmission to Vehicle or operators. Safety constraints take precedence over efficiency targets.' This ensures high safety criticality is honored.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. The Energy Management System (EMS) is a logical subsystem within NGHE Solution that owns the energy efficiency computation, monitoring, and recommendation logic. EMS interfaces with Vehicle (via Telemetry for sensor data ingestion and Tasking for control recommendations), Teleoperation (via Telemetry for operator display), and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert for validation of recommendations).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: EMS ingests power consumption (kW), load mass (kg), and distance traveled (m) from Vehicle sensors via Telemetry (inbound). EMS broadcasts computed energy_per_ton, efficiency status, and optimization recommendations to Teleoperation, Site Manager, and Maintenance via Telemetry (outbound).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: EMS transmits efficiency optimization commands (e.g., speed setpoint, load balancing directives) to Vehicle propulsion control via Tasking interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: EMS submits proposed efficiency recommendations to Safety Services for validation via SafetyAlert (outbound). Safety Services returns validated/rejected recommendations via SafetyAlert (inbound). EMS only transmits approved recommendations to operators and Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: EMS relies on Network Link for reliable communication with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules, especially for real-time telemetry and safety-critical validation messages.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level integration boundary. EMS (energy efficiency logic) is naturally contained within NGHE Solution as a core subsystem. All external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are already defined at NGHE Solution level. Minimal diffusion: EMS logic is cohesive and does not leak into Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services modules. High containment score reflects that EMS is a well-scoped subsystem of NGHE Solution.

Vehicle module owns propulsion, power systems, and onboard sensors. Placing EMS within Vehicle would couple energy efficiency logic tightly to Vehicle hardware and create diffusion: EMS would need to replicate across multiple Vehicle variants or be tightly bound to a single Vehicle design. Additionally, EMS must serve multiple stakeholders (Site Manager, Tele-operator, Maintenance) who are external to Vehicle; embedding EMS in Vehicle would require Vehicle to push data outbound to all stakeholders, violating separation of concerns. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module owns remote operator interface and command transmission. Placing EMS within Teleoperation would couple energy efficiency computation to the teleoperation gateway, making it unavailable to on-site operators and Site Manager. EMS must be independent of operation mode (on-site vs. remote). High diffusion risk: EMS logic would be duplicated or fragmented across on-site and remote control paths. Not recommended.

Safety Services module owns safety validation, hazard monitoring, and safety alerts. Placing EMS within Safety Services would conflate energy efficiency (a performance KPI) with safety (a constraint). While EMS must interact with Safety Services for validation, EMS is not a safety function itself; it is a performance optimization function that must be constrained by safety. Embedding EMS in Safety Services would diffuse safety logic and create confusion about safety vs. performance responsibilities. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Energy Management System (EMS) is a cohesive subsystem that logically belongs within NGHE Solution because: (1) EMS is a system-level capability that integrates data from Vehicle and serves multiple external stakeholders; (2) EMS is independent of operation mode (on-site or remote) and must be accessible to all stakeholders; (3) EMS interfaces with Safety Services as a peer validation function, not as a subordinate; (4) EMS uses well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) that are already established at NGHE Solution level. Containment within NGHE Solution minimizes diffusion and maintains clear separation of concerns.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution module is not yet decomposed into subsystems, EMS ownership may be ambiguous; mitigation: explicitly define EMS as a named subsystem within NGHE Solution architecture.

Risk: If Telemetry interface is not sufficiently standardized, EMS may need custom adapters for each Vehicle variant; mitigation: enforce strict Telemetry interface specification and require all Vehicle implementations to conform.

Risk: If Safety Services validation is slow or unreliable, EMS recommendations may be delayed or rejected, reducing real-time efficiency benefit; mitigation: define SLA for SafetyAlert validation (e.g., <100 ms response time) and implement fallback logic if validation times out.

Risk: If Network Link is unreliable, EMS may lose telemetry data or fail to transmit recommendations; mitigation: implement local buffering and retry logic; define graceful degradation (e.g., revert to conservative efficiency mode if network is down).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define EMS as a named subsystem within NGHE Solution architecture. Document EMS ownership, interfaces, and dependencies. Establish interface specifications for Telemetry (sensor data schema), Tasking (command schema), and SafetyAlert (validation request/response schema) to ensure interoperability across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (centralized Energy Management System) directly addresses the refined FR by providing mechanisms to measure energy consumption, compute energy_per_ton, and deliver optimization recommendations to all stakeholders. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific algorithms or hardware) and is verifiable through telemetry data collection and efficiency metric computation. The DP supports both on-site and tele-operated modes, aligning with the FR's requirement to 'enable operators and tele-operators.' Coupling is acceptable and managed through well-defined interfaces.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: If EMS is not clearly scoped as a subsystem within NGHE Solution, responsibility for energy efficiency may be ambiguous across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; mitigation: document EMS as a named subsystem with explicit ownership.

Diffusion Risk 2: If Telemetry interface is not standardized, EMS may require custom data adapters for each Vehicle variant, leading to code duplication; mitigation: enforce strict Telemetry schema and require all Vehicle implementations to conform.

Diffusion Risk 3: If SafetyAlert validation is not well-defined, EMS recommendations may bypass safety checks or be delayed; mitigation: establish clear SafetyAlert protocol and SLA for validation response time.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EMS is well-contained within NGHE Solution. All external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are already defined at NGHE Solution level. EMS does not require new interfaces or module boundaries. Ownership is clear: NGHE Solution system engineer is responsible for EMS design and integration. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules are consumers/providers of EMS interfaces, not owners of EMS logic.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-003 is feasible through telemetry data collection and post-analysis. Acceptance criteria (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) can be measured directly from EMS output over the agreed evaluation period. Verification does not require new instrumentation or interfaces beyond those already defined (Telemetry, Tasking). Verification can be conducted in controlled trials and field operations.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of CR-NGHE-003 to NGHE Solution is clear. Sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-003.1 through CR-NGHE-003.5) are allocated to specific subsystems: Vehicle owns measurement of energy consumption and tonnage (003.1, 003.2); EMS owns computation and display of energy_per_ton (003.3); EMS owns optimization recommendations (003.4); EMS owns telemetry transmission to stakeholders (003.5). All allocations are traceable to module boundaries and interfaces.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Explicitly define EMS as a named subsystem within NGHE Solution architecture document. Include EMS ownership, responsibilities, and interface specifications.

Revision 2: Establish formal interface specifications for Telemetry (sensor data schema, update frequency, accuracy requirements), Tasking (command schema, latency requirements), and SafetyAlert (validation request/response schema, SLA for response time).

Revision 3: Define fallback and degradation logic for EMS in case of network outage, sensor failure, or Safety Services unavailability. Specify conservative efficiency mode (e.g., revert to baseline efficiency if real-time optimization is unavailable).

Revision 4: Establish acceptance criteria for energy_per_ton measurement accuracy (e.g., ±5% or better). Define sensor calibration and validation procedures to ensure measurement reliability over the evaluation period.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 is not atomic; it requires decomposition into sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-003.1 through CR-NGHE-003.5) to allocate responsibilities across Vehicle, EMS, and Safety Services modules. The refined FR and selected DP are sound and pass all gates. The assigned IM (NGHE Solution with EMS subsystem) is appropriate and well-contained. However, further decomposition is necessary to detail the design of each sub-FR, including sensor specifications (003.1, 003.2), real-time computation algorithms (003.3), optimization logic (003.4), and telemetry transmission protocols (003.5). Decomposition will also clarify the interaction between EMS and Safety Services for validation of efficiency recommendations, which is critical given the high safety criticality constraint.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) from all propulsion and auxiliary systems.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log tonnage moved (mass × distance or equivalent work metric).
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and display real-time energy_per_ton ratio to operators and tele-operators.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide control recommendations or automated efficiency optimizations (e.g., speed modulation, load balancing, idle reduction) to minimize energy_per_ton.
CR-NGHE-003.5: The NGHE solution shall transmit energy efficiency telemetry and alerts to Site Manager and Maintenance stakeholders for performance tracking and predictive maintenance.

assumptions: Vehicle power and load sensors are calibrated and provide ±5% or better accuracy.
Telemetry interface has sufficient bandwidth and latency (<1 second) to support real-time energy_per_ton updates.
Safety Services module can validate EMS recommendations within <100 ms (SLA to be confirmed).
Network Link is reliable enough to support real-time telemetry and safety-critical validation messages; fallback logic is implemented for network outages.
E_target (customer-defined energy efficiency threshold) is specified and agreed upon before evaluation period begins.
Evaluation period is representative of typical worksite conditions and includes both on-site and tele-operated operation modes.
open_risks: Risk: Sensor calibration drift over time may invalidate energy_per_ton measurements; mitigation: implement periodic sensor validation and alert operators to recalibration needs.

Risk: EMS computation may be CPU-intensive and degrade real-time performance under high-frequency telemetry; mitigation: profile and optimize EMS algorithms; consider edge computing on Vehicle if needed.
Risk: Safety Services validation may be slow or unreliable, delaying EMS recommendations; mitigation: define SLA for SafetyAlert validation and implement fallback logic.
Risk: Network Link outage may prevent EMS from transmitting recommendations or receiving telemetry; mitigation: implement local buffering and graceful degradation.

Risk: Efficiency optimization recommendations may conflict with safety constraints (e.g., speed reduction for efficiency may compromise braking); mitigation: Safety Services must validate all EMS recommendations before transmission.
evidence_needed: Sensor specification and calibration data for power and load measurement.
Telemetry interface specification (schema, update frequency, latency requirements).
EMS algorithm design and performance analysis (CPU load, latency, accuracy).
Safety Services validation protocol and SLA for SafetyAlert response time.
Network Link reliability analysis and fallback logic design.
Acceptance criteria for energy_per_ton measurement accuracy (e.g., ±5% or better).
Controlled trial results demonstrating energy_per_ton ≤ E_target under representative worksite conditions.
Field trial results from both on-site and tele-operated operation modes.
